fields.shortDefinition: Authority is recognized standing to direct, decide, or govern within a governance order.
fields.coreMeaning: Within the governance domain, authority functions by attaching directive right to a role, office, or position that others treat as operative. It concerns who may direct, not what can be enforced or whether that standing is valid.
fields.fullDefinition:
Authority is not any ability to produce effects. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as a structured right to issue direction, make decisions, or govern within a governance order.

Its internal composition joins a directive claim to a recognized role, office, or position. The concept requires that the right to direct is treated as attached to standing rather than to mere capacity.

Within governance structures, authority organizes who may decide, command, or settle matters. Questions of whether that standing is valid belong to legitimacy, while questions of what outcomes can be produced belong to power.

fields.shortDefinition: Formal framing: Authority is recognized standing to direct, decide, or govern within a governance order.
fields.coreMeaning: Formal framing: Within the governance domain, authority functions by attaching directive right to a role, office, or position that others treat as operative. It concerns who may direct, not what can be enforced or whether that standing is valid.
fields.fullDefinition:
Formal framing: Authority is not any ability to produce effects. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as a structured right to issue direction, make decisions, or govern within a governance order.

Its internal composition joins a directive claim to a recognized role, office, or position. The concept requires that the right to direct is treated as attached to standing rather than to mere capacity.

Within governance structures, authority organizes who may decide, command, or settle matters. Questions of whether that standing is valid belong to legitimacy, while questions of what outcomes can be produced belong to power.

fields.shortDefinition: Power is effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.
fields.coreMeaning: Within the governance domain, power functions through the ability to produce effects, secure compliance, or alter conditions in relation to a governance structure. It does not depend on recognized right or accepted validity.
fields.fullDefinition:
Power is not identical to authority or legitimacy. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.

Its internal composition may rest in resources, coercive capacity, institutional position, coordination, or control over dependencies. The concept therefore concerns what can be made to happen, not who may rightfully direct or whether that capacity counts as valid.

Within governance structures, power explains operative effect. Authority may channel it and legitimacy may justify it, but neither is required for power to exist or to work.

fields.shortDefinition: Simplified explanation: Power is effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.
fields.coreMeaning: Simplified explanation: Within the governance domain, power functions through the ability to produce effects, secure compliance, or alter conditions in relation to a governance structure. It does not depend on recognized right or accepted validity.

Within governance structures, power explains operative effect. Authority may channel it and legitimacy may justify it, but neither is required for power to exist or to work.

fields.shortDefinition: Formal framing: Power is effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.
fields.coreMeaning: Formal framing: Within the governance domain, power functions through the ability to produce effects, secure compliance, or alter conditions in relation to a governance structure. It does not depend on recognized right or accepted validity.
fields.fullDefinition:
Formal framing: Power is not identical to authority or legitimacy. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.

Its internal composition may rest in resources, coercive capacity, institutional position, coordination, or control over dependencies. The concept therefore concerns what can be made to happen, not who may rightfully direct or whether that capacity counts as valid.

Within governance structures, power explains operative effect. Authority may channel it and legitimacy may justify it, but neither is required for power to exist or to work.

fields.shortDefinition: Legitimacy is accepted or justified validity of rule or standing within a governance order.
fields.coreMeaning: Within the governance domain, legitimacy functions by marking a rule, office, or standing as valid rather than merely operative. It concerns what counts as justified or accepted, not who may direct or what can be made to happen.
fields.fullDefinition:
Legitimacy is not the same as authority or power. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as accepted or justified validity of rule or standing within a governance order.

Its internal composition joins acceptance or justification to a rule, office, institution, or governing order. The concept therefore evaluates standing rather than conferring directive right or operative capacity.

Within governance structures, legitimacy stabilizes obedience, trust, and continuity by making authority or power count as valid. Power may operate without it, and authority may exist while its legitimacy is contested.

fields.fullDefinition:
Formal framing: Legitimacy is not the same as authority or power. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as accepted or justified validity of rule or standing within a governance order.

Its internal composition joins acceptance or justification to a rule, office, institution, or governing order. The concept therefore evaluates standing rather than conferring directive right or operative capacity.

Within governance structures, legitimacy stabilizes obedience, trust, and continuity by making authority or power count as valid. Power may operate without it, and authority may exist while its legitimacy is contested.

fields.shortDefinition: Responsibility is answerable connection between an actor and attributable actions, roles, or outcomes.
fields.coreMeaning: Responsibility functions by linking conduct or effects back to an actor as something for which that actor can be called to account. It concerns answerability for what is attributable, not simply obedience to a required rule.
fields.fullDefinition:
Responsibility is not identical to duty. Duty concerns what must be done under a rule or role; responsibility concerns what an actor can be held answerable for.

Its internal structure joins attribution to answerability. The concept can attach to conduct, omission, stewardship, or resulting state, provided the actor is treated as bearing that connection.

Within a system, responsibility assigns who must answer for actions, decisions, or conditions. It therefore organizes attribution before judgment, sanction, or repair.

fields.fullDefinition:

Its internal structure joins attribution to answerability. The concept can attach to conduct, omission, stewardship, or resulting state, provided the actor is treated as bearing that connection.

Within a system, responsibility assigns who must answer for actions, decisions, or conditions. It therefore organizes attribution before judgment, sanction, or repair.

fields.shortDefinition: Duty is required conduct owed by an actor under a rule, role, or obligation.
fields.coreMeaning: Duty functions by attaching a required line of conduct to a position, office, or binding norm. It concerns what must be done or refrained from, not who is answerable for all resulting outcomes.
fields.fullDefinition:
Duty is not identical to responsibility. Responsibility links an actor to answerability; duty specifies conduct that is owed under a rule, role, office, or obligation.

Its internal structure joins requirement to an actor through a binding source. The concept therefore concerns directive obligation rather than mere preference or capacity.

Within a system, duty stabilizes expected conduct by marking what is required before action occurs. Whether failure produces broader responsibility is a further question, not the definition of duty itself.
